title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered for a community cycling scheme. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Sofia, The Route Buddy
Sofia volunteered as a patient route buddy for adults who wanted to cycle to work but felt nervous in traffic. [47]She began by riding each person’s commute alone, noting junctions where parked cars forced cyclists into the road.[/47] Her background as a calm driving instructor helped her explain road position without sounding reckless. She expected fear to disappear after one successful journey through town, but confidence grew more slowly. [48]For Sofia, the biggest breakthrough came when a nurse who had avoided cycling for years sent a photo of her bike locked outside the hospital.[/48] Sofia learned to celebrate tiny victories, such as signalling clearly or choosing a quieter side street. She also persuaded the council to repaint a faded and confusing lane marking near the station. Sofia says the scheme is not about turning everyone into athletes; it is about helping ordinary people trust their own judgement on familiar roads.
texts.B:
### Martin, The Bike Library Mechanic
Martin worked in the bike library, where residents could borrow refurbished bicycles safely for a full month before deciding whether to buy one. [49]He was shocked by how often donated bikes looked fine but had chains so worn that they slipped under pressure.[/49] He immediately introduced a laminated checklist for brakes, tyres and gears before any bicycle left the workshop. [50]His years repairing farm machinery helped him improvise solutions when standard parts did not fit older donated frames.[/50] The small workshop was cold and badly lit, and his hands ached by the end of winter sessions. Martin enjoyed hearing borrowers return with stories about successful school runs, shopping trips and first rides in decades. He tried hard not to lecture people about maintenance, instead showing them one useful habit at a time. He believes the bike library works because it removes the fear of wasting money on the wrong bicycle.
texts.C:
### Anika, The Training Session Planner
Anika organised free weekend training sessions in the park for local families who wanted safer cycling skills before the summer holidays. [51]She changed the session order after quickly realising children became bored if helmet checks happened before they had ridden anywhere.[/51] Now, instead, the first activity is a silly slow race, which makes balance practice feel like a game. Her experience as a patient primary teacher helped her turn instructions into short, memorable challenges. [52]The hardest part was persuading confident parents to let their children make small mistakes rather than anxiously grabbing the handlebars too soon.[/52] Anika also had to translate tricky cycling vocabulary into simple, colourful diagrams for local families with limited English. She was delighted when a grandmother joined one session only to watch, then borrowed a small bike herself the following month. Anika thinks the training works best when adults and children admit they are learning together.
texts.D:
### Reece, The Parking Campaigner
Reece joined the cycling scheme after his nearly new bike was stolen from outside the library, despite being carefully locked. [53]He photographed every broken stand and badly lit rack in the neighbourhood before writing careful letters to shop owners and local councillors.[/53] At first he often felt like a nuisance, but he knew stolen bikes were one reason people gave up cycling altogether. [54]His most awkward meeting was with a supermarket manager who insisted that customers did not want cycle parking until Reece calmly showed survey results from local shoppers.[/54] Two weeks later, the manager eventually agreed to install six covered stands near the entrance. Reece learned that polite, repeated persistence beat angry emails. [55]He now carefully keeps a public map showing where secure parking exists and where requests are still unanswered by officials.[/55] [56]He was especially pleased when teenagers began using the map to plan rides to football practice.[/56]

questions.question: checked a route personally in order to identify risky points for cyclists?
questions.explanation: Sofia rode each commute alone and noted dangerous junctions.

questions.question: remembers evidence that a previously anxious rider had completed a meaningful journey?
questions.explanation: Sofia received a photo of a nurse’s bike locked outside the hospital.

questions.question: discovered that bicycles which looked acceptable could still fail under force?
questions.explanation: Martin found donated bikes with chains so worn that they slipped under pressure.

questions.question: used experience with other machinery to adapt repairs for old bicycles?
questions.explanation: Martin’s farm machinery experience helped him improvise when parts did not fit older frames.

questions.question: changed the order of activities because young participants lost interest?
questions.explanation: Anika changed the session order after children became bored by helmet checks.

questions.question: had to stop adults from intervening too quickly while children learned?
questions.explanation: Anika found it hard to persuade parents not to grab the handlebars.

questions.question: gathered photographic evidence of poor parking facilities before contacting decision-makers?
questions.explanation: Reece photographed broken stands and badly lit racks before writing to owners and councillors.

questions.question: used survey data to challenge a business owner’s assumption?
questions.explanation: Reece showed a supermarket manager survey results from local shoppers.

questions.question: maintains an online record of available facilities and unresolved requests?
questions.explanation: Reece keeps a public map of secure parking and unanswered requests.

questions.question: was pleased that young people used his resource to organise sports journeys?
questions.explanation: Reece was pleased teenagers used the map to plan rides to football practice.
